item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, it's not anartists wanted to expressed themselves and f██ked up? (as I initially thought)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for a lesbian anithero, even better that this one was a survivor of misogynistic abuse, and such characters enacting revenge by destroying abusers everywhere. one of those myths that I sure wish was real. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love the urban legend theme you're going here. My only problem is that accused witches were never burned in Salem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one. I enjoy the take on something "classically" supernatural in an SCP, especially when it's done this well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for pre 1900s horror; it's obvious that I enjoyed this. This is a fun idea which doesn't need plenty of analysis and depth as it reflects the on the era of history when anomalous arts were outlawed so it doesn't need much explanation. Also being burned from the inside is not a fun way to exit this world. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It still definitely wouldn't be good for you, but some corrections and points of interest:

Ethanoic Acid is more commonly known as acetic acid.
Dilute acetic acid is more commonly known as "vinegar".
The fact that some of the ethanol would probably oxidize to vinegar wouldn't make your day any better, but the more immediate concern would still be the "scalding hot" part.

On a side note, this reaction actually occurs naturally in the liver with the help of catalysts as you digest alcohol. This is why people who have been drinking a lot sometimes smell like pickles.

Another interesting reaction this situation could cause is the formation of Chloral from ethanol and hydrochloric acid, which is a fairly potent old-school sedative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: J-3998-1 isn't very funny at all!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry for necro, but this could mean she is the one responsible for the increased rate of arson and homicides right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for necro, but WTF???

HOLY DAMN.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An amazing twist when I nearly lose interest to the common urban legend.+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love this. I love the pictures and the posters and the police report too!
"boiling ethanol"
Ethanol has a pretty low boiling point at 79 degrees C, so wouldn't cause burns, it would be a lot worse!
If this was to appear in the stomach, you may find yourself actually oxidizing the ethanol! The combination of ethanoic acid and hot ethanol would also dissolve cell membranes in the throat after vomiting! This would not be a fun time!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree that the old documents look blatantly inauthentic. Solving that won't be easy though because someone would have to take some of their time off to handwrite the documents and photoshop them to look authentic.

On an aside note, Candice feels like a Marvel Comics character written today, a flaming lesbian skeleton with no legs that targets murderers that abuse their spouses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: whoops, fixed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now that it's at the 3998 slot, shouldn't the document picture be changed to SCP-3998, instead of SCP-3000?

item.title: SCP-3000 >> SCP-3998
item.content:encoded:
[+] WOOO
[-] Contest was great everybody! Hope y'all had as much fun as I did!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This, definitely. The fact that the setting of this is the Salem Witch Trials doesn't add to the skip, but, in fact, takes away from it, especially given the ridiculous amounts of potential totally wasted. The Salem Witch Trials are the perfect setting, and this uses almost none of what makes the setting good for SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to like this, but I don't. My problems are largely addressed by others — moose talked about how obviously inauthentic the journals and dialogue were; Waterfire noted how fake they looked; Rioghail pointed out the disconnect with the actual Salem witch trials.

I'll toss in a note that "oops, the corpse didn't belong to the character we thought it did, and that one is still on the loose" was done to similar effect not too long ago in the excellent SCP-2632, so it didn't do much for me this time around.

And the thought of unsympathetic people rapidly burning to death just doesn't get me very interested.

I like the idea of a heroic lesbian witch given new life by her devilesque lover, but it's not enough to surmount my other problems with the piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this article, and can I just say that first image is wonderful. This is great!

+1

AAAaaaaa Just noticed that this hit 100+ upvotes!!!

THANK U;w;U

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was a little torn on this one. The journal article and dialogue exchanges are not very good or authentic — maybe because I grew up reading quite a few actual journals from that time period (I don't see that brought up in earlier comments). And the effect is disappointingly straightforward.

However, I really really loved the twist with the devil falling in love with Candice, and the queer element, and the final twist about Candice's real fate. And beyond the dialogue/journal issues, it's very well executed.

On balance, I loved the parts which I loved more than I disliked the parts I disliked. Upvoted, and well done.

EDIT: Oh, nice. I'm the 100th upvote. Congrats!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only gripe is with the images. It's pretty clear to see that it's a cursive font and not handwriting, which is somewhat distracting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Horrible" like "awful", "awesome", "terrible', or "terrific" has lost its original meaning, and doesn't mean what you seem to think it means, but rather just means "bad".

Although it is interesting that unlike "awful/awesome" or "terrible/terrific", "horrible/horrific" while no longer synonymous are both negative superlatives. Horrific is the only one that retains its original meaning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, a lesbian super anti-heroine! Very political right.Welcome to join the super contained league!

Honestly, this one was not horrible at all. Not suitble for the 3k contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1. Great job on this. Really really enjoyed
the twist
– hide block

the idea that its Aiden who catches fire each time she's out there exacting vengeance still. Nice switch on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: they mean "made due", in L-3000-4.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you were around for the 1k contest, this would have been perfect as well.

The only thing I wasn't a fan of was the transcript between the judge and Candice; the dialog felt stiff (unusually so), but it also feels nessecary to give her a voice in the article as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm trying to find where it says "make do" but can't. Sorry, could you tell me where it is? U-^-;U

Also, this is true, and an intentional part of the skip. U>w0U

Glad you enjoyed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pedant point - I'm almost certain that the phrase is 'make do', not 'make due'.
This would have been the only witch burned at Salem.

Definitely horrific.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh.

Part of me wants to say this feels like a 1000 contest skip that made its way into the 3000 contest due to the urban legend aspect at play here. That aside, this was a creepy article that makes good use of the period it is set in, and the twist at the end definitely caught me off guard. I found it more creepy than out right horror, but still chilling enough to warrant a +1.

Nice job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. I feel it's good enough to stand on its own, but I'm not sure if it falls into the 'horror' category
Spoiler
Hide Spoiler

… unless you count the 'horror' of Aiden being presumably burning forever in Candice's hell.

This also adds an interesting addition to Clovis' character. Not quite depth, but just a neat thing to note.

+1 because I'm a sucker for Pitch Haven.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

The body was Aiden. Candice is still around killing fools.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't exactly horror — maybe meant to leave the reader aghast at the treatment of women in the 17th century, but it left no effect on me even so — and the poor EME was too much to take. The story it tells is good, if not exactly original, but I'm at a loss as to what the reveal of the body not being Candice is supposed to suggest, and the legend reveals nothing the article itself doesn't already suggest. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
About Pitch Haven.. Somehow one of the ████ residents are involved in the shenanigans.

+ Click to spoil
- Very well.

Look at the letter.. It's from..?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
weirdly enough this reminds me of pitch haven and ghost rider. just wut.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've upvoted this, but making this an actual part of the Salem witch trials seems on the nose and a little bit tacky. There's no real engagement with what happened (this trial almost seems like it occurs in a vacuum) and so it ends up as set dressing chosen because it was famous.

EDIT: Having re-read it after the contest concluded, I've decided to reverse my vote.

item.title: Re: Author post
item.content:encoded:
Thank you. Someone did a witch, even if I couldn't make mine work.

A genuine thank you from the bottom of my heart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh okay. Didn't realize that. Sorry

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Works for me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, hmm… I reworded it a bit. How's it now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I posted that before she changed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
self-defense and manslaughter are exempted, but what about the person who signs off on their spouse's DNR? Or the person who survives the car wreck that kills their spouse? Or unknowingly serves the contaminated salad? Or similar causes of death that aren't criminal at all?

Most of those sound accidental, and it doesn't say manslaughter specifically, it just says in an accident (footnote), so it's a broader term than manslaughter. Also, with the DNR, the people they apply to usually request those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it in chat, and I like it here. It's just a really enjoyable read. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoy the melancholy tone here, but I feel that a more thorough proofreading before going live would have served it well. I noticed numerous typos, which I pointed out to you in chat. I liked the legend note, but I felt like by the end it hadn't really reached a satisfactory conclusion. Still, I liked the story being told here and enjoyed the varied media formats on display. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well I probably should have asked this in the draft stage, but I got fixated on helping with the early modern English; self-defense and manslaughter are exempted, but what about the person who signs off on their spouse's DNR? Or the person who survives the car wreck that kills their spouse? Or unknowingly serves the contaminated salad? Or similar causes of death that aren't criminal at all?

Also I wonder about the manslaughter thing, since many (most?) domestic murders aren't premeditated and often prosecuted as (or plea bargained) to manslaughter. Is the intent here to exempt these?

Also Aiden and Candice Hayes are more dammed "A" and "C" names! Are these characters referenced in any other Pitch Haven material? (My poor chart is like spider web now).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
heck

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So glad this is up! It's an excellent piece of time-period-specific horror, with some tragedy in there too.

item.title: Author post
item.content:encoded:
Hey y'all! Glad to be a last-minute participant!

Thanks to all of y'all who looked this over in chat, you guys were a big help!

Credit for the first image goes to Marrow, of House of Marrow

Credit for the foreword image goes to SunnyClockwork.

Thanks so much for reading!! Hope ya enjoyed!
